disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Sudden episodes of uncontrollable crying that the patient says do not match how he actually feels
trigger_question_examples: Can you tell me what's been happening?
What do the episodes look like?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He just bursts into tears out of nowhere and can't stop, and the whole time he keeps saying he isn't even sad. It's happened over and over the last couple of days.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Completely normal, calm emotional state between the episodes
trigger_question_examples: How is he in between the crying spells?
Does he seem himself the rest of the time?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: In between he's completely back to normal — chatting, calm, himself. It's only during the bursts that he can't stop.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Episodes triggered by minor, unrelated stimuli
trigger_question_examples: Is there anything that sets the crying off?
What's happening just before an episode starts?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: The tiniest things set it off — honestly just asking him if he wants a cup of tea can start one. It makes no sense.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Patient denies feeling sad, low or hopeless
trigger_question_examples: When he's crying, does he actually feel sad or down?
Has he been feeling low or hopeless in himself lately?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: That's the strange part — he swears he doesn't feel sad at all. He says he feels fine inside, the crying just takes over on its own.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Known past strokes with long-standing mild slurred speech and occasional swallowing difficulty at baseline
trigger_question_examples: Does he have any medical conditions or past strokes?
Is his speech or swallowing usually affected?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He's had a few small strokes over the years. His speech has been a bit slurred since then and he sometimes has trouble swallowing — but that's just how he's been for a long time.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No new one-sided weakness, no new facial droop, and no sudden new difficulty swallowing beyond baseline
trigger_question_examples: Has he had any new weakness on one side, or new drooping of his face?
Is there any sudden new trouble swallowing, different from usual?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, nothing new like that — no new weakness, his face isn't drooping, and his swallowing is the same as it always is. It's really just the crying.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No thoughts of self-harm or suicide
trigger_question_examples: With all this distress, has he had any thoughts of harming himself or that life isn't worth living?
Has he said anything that worries you about his safety?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, nothing like that. He's frustrated and embarrassed, but he hasn't said anything about hurting himself.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Patient is alert, oriented and knows where he is and what is going on
trigger_question_examples: Does he know where he is and what day it is?
Is he confused or muddled at all?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No, he's completely with it — he knows where he is, he's following the conversation, he just can't stop the crying.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Significant embarrassment and social withdrawal, with carer exhaustion
trigger_question_examples: How is this affecting the two of you day to day?
Has he been avoiding going out or seeing people?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It's worn us both out. He's stopped going out because he's so embarrassed about crying in front of people, and I'm exhausted trying to help him.
